coredata.dc:title: Bare undoped nontoxic carbon dots as a visible light photocatalyst for the degradation of methylene blue and congo red
coredata.dc:creator: Abbasi, Ambreen
Abushad, M.
Khan, Arisha
Bhat, Zia Ul Haq
Hanif, Summaiya
Shakir, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: Rampant discharge of industrial wastes containing non-biodegradable colored dyes to water bodies has prompted the development of an environment-friendly photocatalytic process using nontoxic, photostable water soluble photocatalyst. In this work, bare undoped carbon dots (CDots) based photocatalyst has been reported. The carbon dots were synthesized via the pyrolysis method using a single source, ammonium citrate; the reported carbon dots were characterized using HR-TEM, XRD, FTIR, XPS, Photoluminescence (P.L.), TGA, and UV–Vis spectroscopy. The catalytic behavior of the synthesized carbon dots was tested against two water-soluble industrial dyes (Methylene Blue (M.B.) and Congo Red (C.R.)). The synthesized carbon dots exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation facilitating 96% degradation of MB within 55 min and 98% degradation of C.R. within 60 min. A plausible mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of studied dyes was proposed based on comprehensive active species trapping experiments. The photogenerated holes (h+) and superoxide radicals (O2 .−) were the main active species responsible for the degradation of studied dyes. The kinetic studies revealed that the cationic methylene blue dye degradation by BUCDs followed the modified Freundlich kinetic model with rate constant 0.063 and anionic congo red dye depicted closed correlation with both parabolic diffusion model and modified Freundlich kinetic model with rate constants 0.256 and 0.196. The synthesized BUCDs showed no cytotoxicity in HCT human colon cancer cell lines, thus establishing their nontoxic nature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bare undoped carbon dots
HCT 116 cancer cell
Non-cytotoxic
Visible light photocatalyst
Methylene blue
Congo red

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic degradation study of methylene blue (M.B.) and congo Red (C.R.) under visible light irradiation
Kinetics modeling of dye degradation studies
The mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (M.B.) and congo red (C.R.) by CDots

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the Department of Chemistry, AMU, assisted through UGC DRS–SAP, DST–PURSE, and DST–FIST programs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDots, scale bar 20 nm (b) size histogram and (c) SAED image with diffuse rings, scale bar 0.001 nm Dif.
(a) Survey spectrum, (b) Deconvoluted spectrum of C1s, (c)) Deconvoluted spectrum of O1s.
Photodegradation plots of (a) M.B. and (b) C.R. under different conditions.
Plausible photodegradation pathway M.B. and C.R. by CDots under visible light irradiation.
Kinetic parameters of different models for degradation of M.B. and C.R. dyes by CDots.

head.ce:title: Bare undoped nontoxic carbon dots as a visible light photocatalyst for the degradation of methylene blue and congo red

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Inorganic Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh 202002, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDivision of Inorganic Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh 202002, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh 202002, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Physics, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh 202002, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rampant discharge of industrial wastes containing non-biodegradable colored dyes to water bodies has prompted the development of an environment-friendly photocatalytic process using nontoxic, photostable water soluble photocatalyst. In this work, bare undoped carbon dots (CDots) based photocatalyst has been reported. The carbon dots were synthesized via the pyrolysis method using a single source, ammonium citrate; the reported carbon dots were characterized using HR-TEM, XRD, FTIR, XPS, Photoluminescence (P.L.), TGA, and UV–Vis spectroscopy. The catalytic behavior of the synthesized carbon dots was tested against two water-soluble industrial dyes (Methylene Blue (M.B.) and Congo Red (C.R.)). The synthesized carbon dots exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation facilitating 96% degradation of MB within 55 min and 98% degradation of C.R. within 60 min. A plausible mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of studied dyes was proposed based on comprehensive active species trapping experiments. The photogenerated holes (h+) and superoxide radicals (O2 .−) were the main active species responsible for the degradation of studied dyes. The kinetic studies revealed that the cationic methylene blue dye degradation by BUCDs followed the modified Freundlich kinetic model with rate constant 0.063 and anionic congo red dye depicted closed correlation with both parabolic diffusion model and modified Freundlich kinetic model with rate constants 0.256 and 0.196. The synthesized BUCDs showed no cytotoxicity in HCT human colon cancer cell lines, thus establishing their nontoxic nature.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Urban water contamination has worsened with the growth of social structure and broad areas of industrial production [1]. The extensive dumping of carcinogenic industrial effluents and colored dyes from various industries, such as textiles, paper, medications, and beauty products, has become health threats to livestock, plant lives, and humans [2,3]. Dyes have become a significant source of water pollution, with 1–15% of dyes being lost as pollutants during the dying process [4]. It is well known that these dyes are extensively used as they are resistant to sunlight and oxidation by microorganisms due to their complex chemical structure [5]. Depending on their structure, dyes are classified into cations, anions, and azo dyes. The presence of dyes such as methylene blue, Congo red, and other textile dyes causes severe health issues by adversely affecting the liver, skin, and DNA by forming active toxic adducts [6]. For these reasons, removing these water pollutants from wastewater has become imminent [7]. Biological methods such as aerobic, sedimentation, coagulation-flocculation, adsorption, photo-Fenton process [8], and advanced oxidation processes are employed to remove and degrade these toxic pollutants. Amongst these processes, advanced oxidation via photocatalytic degradation is preferred over other methods. This method is less costly, involves the complete removal of dyes with no production of secondary pollutants, and is eco-friendly [9]. The dominating factor involving photocatalytic reactions is the structure of the electronic band and band energy. It is well known that the photocatalytic reaction rate is enhanced with the help of a photocatalyst, which should possess a high surface area, be free of toxicity, be cost-effective, and not form any toxic and hazardous by products [10].

Carbon Dots (CDots) are carbon nanoparticles less than 10 nm in size. CDots may have a crystalline or an amorphous carbon core depending on the origin. CDots with crystalline carbon core have sp2 carbon, and if originated from graphite as precursor, the CDots may retain the (002) stacking periodicity of graphite. Amorphous CDots contain sp2 and sp3 carbon in their core. Both crystalline and amorphous CDots have surface functional groups that provide them with stabilization [11]. Carbon dots have numerous amorphous and nanocrystalline core mainly comprised of sp2 carbon, with graphitic lattice space and numerous functional groups on their surface, which impart excellent water solubility and the possibility of further functionalization [12]. The unique properties of carbon dots, such as light-harvesting ability and bright photoluminescence, excellent photostability, high aqueous solubility, photosensitization, and efficient photoexcited electron transfer enable their use in photocatalysis where generated holes and electrons do the job [13–15]. In addition, due to up-converted photoluminescence, carbon dots can promote two or more low-energy photons to higher energy protons by subsequent absorption of longer wavelength multiphoton, which leads to light emission at a shorter wavelength than the exciting wavelength. This property expands the light utilization range of carbon dots, improves photoinduced electron transfer, and allows the design of an efficient visible light-responsive photocatalyst [16].

Carbon dots play an essential role in photodegradation, and their role can be broadly divided as (a) Carbon dots as photocatalysts, (b) Carbon dots as photosensitizers, (c) Carbon dots as electron acceptor/ mediators, and (d) Carbon dots as up-conversion materials [13].

Bella Nathanael Jusuf et al. have reported the photodegradation of methylene blue under sunlight irradiation by carbon dots synthesized from the eggshell membrane [17]. Sima Umrao et al. have reported visible light photodegradation of methylene blue by multi-layered graphene quantum dots [18]. Lu Shuang Li et al. have reported a composite of N, S, and P- tridoped carbon dots and TiO2 as a U.V. light catalyst for methylene blue degradation. In their work, they reported the composite of carbon dots and TiO2 was a better photocatalyst than pristine TiO2 [19] . Moorthy Maruthapandi et al. have used composites of polyaniline and nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots synthesized from bovine serum albumin as visible light photocatalysts for the degradation of congo red and methylene blue. In addition, they reported complete degradation of congo red within 20 mins, whereas methylene blue required a longer treatment time. The combination of holes (h*), hydroxyl (OH .
−-), and superoxide (O2
.
−) were the notable species required in the degradation of dyes [20].

Numerous pieces of literature have been reported where heteroatom-doped carbon dots and their composites are used as photocatalysts for degrading organic dyes. However, reports in which bare undoped carbon dots act as efficient visible light photocatalysts are rare. Hu et al. applied an acid reflux-assisted method to synthesize carbon dots and used them for methylene blue degradation [21]. Carbon dots synthesized from coal only were used for methylene blue degradation [22]. Das et al. have reported green emitting carbon dots synthesized from pear juice and had shown their photocatalytic activity under visible light towards the degradation of methylene blue [23]. Zaib et al. used leaves of Elettaria Cardamomum to synthesize carbon dots and then used these dots to degrade congo red and methylene blue [24].

In this paper, we have synthesized bare undoped carbon dots using a single precursor, i.e., diammonium citrate, and employed a one-step pyrolysis method to synthesize these carbon dots. These carbon dots were further used to degrade congo red and methylene blue under visible light illumination.

All chemicals used were of analytical grade. Ammonium Citrate, Methylene Blue and Congo red were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific India Pvt. Double distil water used throughout the study was prepared in the chemical laboratory at AMU.

The Hitachi F-2700 spectrometer was used to record fluorescence spectra. Electronic spectral studies were done on a Perkin Elmer Lamda 365 UV–VIS spectrometer. FT-IR spectrum was obtained from Perkin Elmer Spectrum Two FT-IR Spectrometer. TEM was carried out in a JEM-2100F JEOL, Japan operating at the maximum accelerating voltage of 200 kV. For this, 5 µL of the sample was drop coated on a carbon-coated copper TEM grid followed by air drying. Acquired micrographs were analyzed using ImageJ (http://imagej.nih.gov/ij/download.html) by randomly selecting individual nanoparticles in the TEM images to compute size distribution histograms. X-ray diffraction experiments were carried out using SMARTLAB Rigaku with Cu-Kα radiation. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were done on HORIBA Jobin Yvon Fluorocube-01-NL fluorescence lifetime by using Time correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) method. Thermal gravimetric analysis of carbon dots was determined using Mettler Toledo Thermogravimetric analyzer with built in gas controller (TGA2 SF/1100) and fitted with XP1U TGA balance (ultra-micro balance), in the temperature range 25–800 °C under nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 100C/min. For data analysis and graphs plotting, ORIGIN PRO software was used. PHI 5000 VersaProbe III X-ray Photoelectron spectrometer using Al Kα X-ray source was used for XPS characterization. A Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, U.K.) was used to determine the zeta potential of CDots at pH 7.

The CDots were synthesised accordingly to reported method [25] with little modification. 2 gm Ammonium citrate dibasic was taken in 250 ml beaker and was kept in muffle furnace and heated up to 190 °C and was kept at that temperature for half an hour. After half an hour the black residue obtained was dissolved in 30 ml distilled water and was subjected to centrifugation for 20 min. The supernatant obtained was than subjected to dialysis (MWCO=1000D) for 6 h. The purified solution was kept in oven at 60 °C to obtain CDots powder. The weight of obtained CDots was 1.2 gm.

The photocatalytic activity of synthesized CDots was carried out for the degradation of methylene blue (MB) and Congo red (CR) dyes in aqueous solution with the irradiation of visible light. The MB and CR dyes are very common industrial waste pollutants and resistant to light and so these dyes were selected as model pollutants. The respective organic dyes (MB and CR) solutions were prepared in a definite concentration with double distill water. In a photochemical reactor, the prepared solution, as well CDots (which act as photocatalysts), are irradiated, that are made up of pyrex glass consisting with triple jacket, visible light tungsten halogen lamp (500 mW) connected with power supply, magnetic stirrer and oxygen pump for the supply of atmospheric oxygen to the sample during performing of the experiments. To keep the constant temperature (20±0.5 0C) of the MB and CR dyes solution, the photoreactor was surrounded by cold water. The calculated amount of aqueous solution (300 ml) of organic pollutants (MB and CR dyes, 5 ppm) was poured inside the photoreactor, then 0.3 gm/ ml of prepared catalyst was added to initiate the visible light irradiated photocatalytic process. The stoichiometric amount of prepared mixture was continuous stirred for 30 min before the illumination with visible light so that the adsorption and desorption equilibrium of solution can be reached. After that, the visible light source was irradiated to the solution mixture and 5 ml of sample was withdrawn with the help of syringe in a particular interval of time during illumination. The process of analysis was carried out afterwards after removal of catalyst from the solution using a centrifuge. The following formula is employed to calculate the degradation efficiency of the photocatalyst
Degradation Efficiency = (A0-A)/A0 100%
where, A0 is the initial absorbance and A is the final absorbance of studied dyes at a particular time (t) respectively, before and after irradiation of visible light.

HCT116 a human colon cancer cell line was grown in RPMI medium supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% (v/v) Penicillin–Streptomycin solution and grown in a humidified environment in 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C. For experiments, HCT 116 cells were seeded into 96-well tissue culture plates at a density of 10,000 cells/well in 5% serum containing growth medium and incubated overnight in CO2 incubator at 37 °C. Adhered cells were then treated with vehicle (1% DMSO) and CDots at various concentrations of 10, 20, 40 and 80 µg/ml, respectively for 48 h. After 48 h of treatment, cell viability was determined by the tetrazolium salt 3-[4,5- dimethylthiazol-2-yl]−2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT; Sigma, St. Louis, MO, USA) assay as described previously [26]. The optical density was measured spectrophotometrically at 540 nM, and results are expressed as a percentage of surviving cells compared with the control [26].

TEM analysis was used to investigate the morphology and size of synthesized carbon dots. TEM image (Fig. 1 ) revealed well-dispersed uniform black dots with spherical morphology. The average diameter of CDots was 1.57 nm with particle size distribution in the range of 0.5–3 nm as evaluated from statistical distribution. In the SAED image (Fig. 1) of the CDots, Diffuse rings were visible, indicative of amorphous carbon nature.
Fig. 2 depicted a broad XRD diffraction peak centered around 24°, attributable to the highly disordered carbon [27].

FT-IR was used to probe any characteristic functional group. FT-IR spectrum is given in Fig. 1 S. The characteristic peaks at 3336 cm−1 are ascribed to stretching vibrations of the OH- group. The peaks at 1584 cm−1 are assigned to -C = C stretching vibration. The peak at 1710 cm−1 corresponded to -C = O stretching vibrations [28]. XPS analysis was conducted to ascertain the functional groups' nature and the carbon dots' chemical composition. XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3 a) revealed to dominant peaks at 284.37 eV (C1s) and 531.5 eV (O1s), respectively [23] with elemental composition of 65.93% and 34.04%. High-resolution C1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into four surface components (Fig. 3b), corresponding to sp2 C (C = C) at binding energy 284.5 eV, COH at binding energy 286.1 eV, C = O at binding energy 287.25 eV, and -COO− binding energy at 288.2 eV [29]. The XPS O1s were deconvoluted into two components (Fig. 3c). The binding energy peaks at 531 eV and 532 eV are consistent with acetate-like O atoms and O-containing polymeric structures [30].

The abundance of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on the surface of CDots was further supported by negative Zeta potential value measured at pH 7 Fig. 2 S. TGA analysis was undertaken to establish the thermal stability of CDots. The TGA curve in Fig. 3 S demonstrated the thermal stability of carbon dots. The thermogram of CDots exhibited a three-step degradation pattern. An initial 2% weight loss at 100 °C may be attributed to the loss of water molecules or moisture content associated with CDots. Further, 4% weight loss was observed between 100 °Cand 268 °C, suggesting thermal stability of carbon dots up to 268° The final degradation took place in the range of 268 °C −739 °C resulting in 94% weight loss, probably due to the cleavage of functional groups present on the surface and CDots. The final TGA curve is that of carbon burn off and no residue is left. The characteristic D and G bands in the Raman spectrum could not be observed (Fig. 4
S) in the present case, probably because the Raman signals of synthesized carbon dots were concealed by intense fluorescence in the background [31].

Fig. 5
S shows the absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of CDots. In the absorption spectrum, a band at 338 nm and a hump at 237 nm was observed, which could be ascribed to the n-π* transition of the C = O bond and π-π* transition of the C = C bond of the aromatic π system [32]. The excitation spectrum of carbon dots showed three peaks at 253 nm, 303 nm, and 360 nm. This behavior indicated that more than one type of excitation energy is trapped on the CD's surface. The direct band gap of 2.6 eV (Fig. 6
S) was calculated for CDots, which was suitable for visible light photocatalysis. As shown in Fig. 7
S, when the excitation wavelength changed from 280 nm to 360 nm, the emission wavelength at 444 nm remained unchanged. Increasing the excitation wavelength from 370 nm to 420 nm brought a red shift in emission wavelength with a subsequent decrease in fluorescent intensity.

The fluorescent quantum yield was 20.45%, with quinine sulfate as the standard. Fig. 8
S shows the lifetime decay profile of C.D.s with tri-exponential decay kinetics. The 10 ns lifetime was calculated from the decay profile.

The effect of pH on the fluorescent intensity was studied by adjusting pH with 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH solutions (Fig. 9S). It was observed that the increase in pH values from pH 3 to pH 9 increased the fluorescent intensity of CDots; beyond pH 9 fluorescent intensity of CDots decreased with an increase in pH values. The decrease in fluorescent intensity can be due to conversion of monodisperse CDots to nanoaggregates at higher pH resulting in weaker fluorescence [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation study of methylene blue (M.B.) and congo Red (C.R.) under visible light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: Two types of water-soluble dyes, i.e., cationic methylene blue and anionic congo red, were used as model pollutants to access the photocatalytic efficiency of synthesized CDots under visible light irradiation. The degradation study of both the dyes was conducted in the presence of 0.3 mg/ml of CDots and 5 mg/L dyes concentration. The solution exhibited high turbidity when CDots concentration was increased from 0.3 mg/ml, which led to a decrease in light penetration, thereby leading to less effective degradation of dyes. The solutions with dye concentration higher than 5 mg/L had absorbance values higher than 1, resulting in low light transmittance and further decreased dye degradation efficiency (data not given). Another factor for the effective degradation of the dyes was the pH of the solution. pH 4 was the most suitable pH to carry out the degradation study of congo red. It has been reported in the literature [34] that at and above pH 4, congo red is known to generate negatively charged polar groups (R-SO3 −), which enhance the degradation of dye by establishing electrostatic interaction with carbon dots acting as the photocatalyst, which becomes positively charged in acidic conditions and negatively charged in alkaline conditions. pH 8 was the optimum pH to carry out the degradation study of methylene blue. The reason for this observation was attributed to the fact that methylene blue predominantly exists as cationic species at pH greater than 6, so at pH 8, there will be a higher electrostatic attraction between methylene blue cationic species and negatively charged carbon dots, leading to effective degradation of dyes [35]. However, dye degradation significantly decreased with an increase in pH beyond pH 8. The reason for this observation was the excess hydroxyl radicals generated at higher pH started competing with dye molecules for the reactive photogenerated reactive oxygen species [36].

The photocatalytic degradation of both dyes was assessed by observing the change in absorbance values with reaction time. No change in the absorbance for both dyes was observed when these dyes were irradiated with visible light in the absence of a photocatalyst, indicating that both the dyes were resistant to visible light (Fig. 4a and b). Under dark conditions with BUCDs, only a slight decrease in the absorbance value was observed both for M.B. and C.R. after 60 mins with dyes degradation efficiency of around 10% (Fig. 4a and b). However, when dyes were irradiated with visible light in the presence of CDots as photocatalysts, a continuous decrease in absorbance with reaction time was observed for both the dyes, with photodegradation efficiency of 96% within 55 min for methylene blue and 98% for congo red within 60 min indicating almost complete degradation of both the dyes (Fig. 4a and b). Finally, the inactivity of CDots towards congo red and methylene blue in the dark strongly suggested that the process is photocatalytic degradation rather than adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics modeling of dye degradation studies
ce:section.ce:para: To get insight into the kinetics of degradation of the studied dyes by photocatalyst (CDots), four different kinetic models (zero order, pseudo-first order, parabolic diffusion, and modified Freundlich models) were fitted on the experimental data.
Zero- Order: At- A0 = -kt
Pseudo-First Order: ln A/ A0 = kt
Parabolic Diffusion: (1-A/A0)/ T = kt−0.5 + a
Modified Freundlich model: log (1-A/A0) = log k + b logt

In all equations, A0 and A are initial and final absorbance, t denotes time, and k denotes the rate constant. Table 1 , respectively, gives the correlation coefficient and rate constant values for different models for the dyes. Figs. 5 and 6 give plots of different models for the dyes

As is evident from Table 1, In the case of methylene blue, R2 values for the modified Freundlich model fitted best amongst the other models. In contrast, for Congo red parabolic diffusion model and modified Freundlich models both fitted adequately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (M.B.) and congo red (C.R.) by CDots
ce:section.ce:para: The -C = O and -COOH groups present on the surface of CDots have been reported to cause higher upward band bending which provides larger internal electric field that facilitates the separation of the electron- hole pairs and induces faster carrier migration, thereby producing higher photocatalytic activity [21]. It has been reported that the oxygen rich surface promotes the adsorption capacity of CDots. The higher the density of oxygen containing groups, the higher the porosity, hydrophilicity and probability of ionic/electronic interaction with methylene blue components which is reflected in their higher photocatalytic performance [37–43]. The plausible degradation mechanism of M.B. and C.R. dyes by CDots under visible light irradiation has been explained by active species trapping experiments. It is cited in the literature [28] that there are mainly three types of reactive species generated by a photocatalyst under light irradiation, i.e., holes, hydroxyl radicals, and superoxide radicals which are actively involved in the photodegradation of dye [44]. To better understand the specific roles of these reactive species, active species trapping experiments were performed using three different types of commonly used scavengers for these species [5]; 5 mM benzoquinone (p-BZQ) (as superoxide scavenger), 5 mM Na2EDTA (as holes scavengers) and 5 mM tert‑butanol (t-BA) (as hydroxyl scavengers) as shown in (Fig. 7a and b), the introduction of t-BA, Na2EDTA, and p-BZQ suppressed the degradation efficiencies to 70%, 30%, and 25% in the case of M.B. and the case of C.R. to 75%, 26%, and 23% indicating that for both the dyes holes and superoxide radicals are playing a significant role in the degradation process. In contrast, the role of hydroxyl radicals is not that significant. [45].

Fig. 10S represents the total organic carbon (TOC) values of M.B. and C.R. dyes after 60 min degradation period. TOC values help estimate the degree of decomposition of organic dyes as their values give the number of organic substances present in the solution. TOC values of M.B. and C.R. were found to be 15.3% and 13.4% signifying that the decrease in absorbance values in the degradation study was not attributed to a simple decolorization process but was instead due to the degradation of respective dyes.

Because of the above results, Fig. 8 represents the plausible pathway for degrading dyes by CDots when exposed to visible light irradiation. According to the depiction, when visible light shines on BUCDs, electrons from the valence band (V.B.) are transferred to the conduction band (C.B.), leaving behind photogenerated holes (h +) in the valance band. The photogenerated electrons in the conduction band are captured by adsorbed O2, generating highly active superoxide radicals (O2
.
−) capable of interacting with dye molecules and degrading them. Finally, the holes interacted with dyes and oxidized them.

One of the prerequisites of a promising photocatalyst is its nontoxic nature. To assess the non-toxicity of the synthesized CDots MTT assay was carried out. HCT 116 cancer cell lines were chosen as a representative cell lines to incubate with CDots for 24 h. Fig. 11S shows that the cell viability remained at almost 100% when the concentrations of CDots reached 80 ug/ml. No morphological change was observed in treating cancer cells with CDots, as shown in Fig 12S; these results suggested that the reported CDots had no cytotoxic effect on the cancer cells.

This work reported the synthesis of bare undoped, nontoxic carbon dots using ammonium citrate as the sole precursor via pyrolysis. The prepared carbon dots were characterized using HR-TEM, XRD, FTIR, photoluminescence (P.L.), TGA, and UV–Vis spectroscopy techniques. The synthesized bare undoped C.D.s were used as a photocatalyst to efficiently degrade two water soluble, highly toxic carcinogenic, and light resistant dyes, viz. methylene blue (M.B.) and congo red (C.R.) under visible light irradiation. The degradation efficiency for MB was 96%, and for C.R., it was 98%. The active species trapping experiments indicated that superoxide radicals and holes mainly carried out the degradation of dyes. The as-prepared CDots were found to have no cytotoxic effect on HCT116 cancer cells, signifying their nontoxic nature.

Ambreen Abbasi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. M. Abushad: Investigation. Arisha Khan: Writing – review & editing. Zia Ul Haq Bhat: Writing – review & editing. Summaiya Hanif: Writing – review & editing. Mohammad Shakir: Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Department of Chemistry, AMU, assisted through UGC DRS–SAP, DST–PURSE, and DST–FIST programs.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cartre.2022.100238.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green fabrication of zinc oxide supported carbon dots for visible light-responsive photocatalytic decolourization of Malachite Green dye: optimization and kinetic studies
Optik (Stuttg)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Sekar, R. Yadav, Green fabrication of zinc oxide supported carbon dots for visible light-responsive photocatalytic decolourization of Malachite Green dye: optimization and kinetic studies, Optik (Stuttg). 242 (2021) 167311. https://doi.org/10.1016/J.IJLEO.2021.167311.

Bioresour. Technol.

Water Sci. Technol.

Front. Chem.
